content: It looks like you were right @Wedson Almeida Filho. :) After playing around with the Milliseconds type, I think that Duration is just a better abstraction for the msleep functionality after considering all of @Miguel Ojeda's feedback. Any objection to me removing the Milliseconds, etc. types and switching back to Duration?

I made the requested revisions here to the PR but I'm wondering what the general stance is on core::ffi::c_uint vs u32. Are these identical on all platforms supported by Rust? I'm mostly wondering stylistically whether it's better to pass a core::ffi::c_uint or u32 to FFI methods since c_uint is just a typedef for u32 on all architectures I'm aware of. I understand that for abstractions, we don't want to use the core::ffi types, but is there a preference when interfacing with FFI?

John Baublitz said:

I made the requested revisions here to the PR but I'm wondering what the general stance is on core::ffi::c_uint vs u32. Are these identical on all platforms supported by Rust? I'm mostly wondering stylistically whether it's better to pass a core::ffi::c_uint or u32 to FFI methods since c_uint is just a typedef for u32 on all architectures I'm aware of. I understand that for abstractions, we don't want to use the core::ffi types, but is there a preference when interfacing with FFI?

I think for FFI, core::ffi::c_uinit is better, as its name suggests, it's a type for FFI ;-)

If you have a Rust-side field of a structure, it's better to use u32.

Just my two cents.
Okay, that's what I did in my PR and I figured that was probably the way to go. :)

I think it depends on what the C type is. If the C type is unsigned int, yeah, use c_uint. But if the C side is using u32/uint32_t we can use u32. Which suggests to me that there's benefit in a patch suggesting use of a sized type on the C side. :)
yes, I'm mainly referring to the "portable types" in C. the sized types obviously have an exact correlation.
c_uint might be preferred for general code, but in kernel pretty much in all cases unsigned int means u32 and unsigned long means usize.
